posts.name: Paul Nute
posts.cooked: All things being equal who makes a better wine — an outstanding producer with so so grapes or a so so producer with outstanding grapes?

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked: @William_Kelley

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
We don’t need to call in William for this one :). He is plenty busy at the moment. (Although if you search his historical posts you’ll find some gems).

Paul, lots of older threads on this topic. We all say Producer. Lots of examples of underwhelming grand cru wines out there in the world. And amazing village wines. And a spectrum of wines in between.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Producer producer producer

posts.name: Paul Nute
posts.cooked: Wow, oh I should have done some searches before posting, sorry!

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
(Chuckles)

Some times the new asking brings out some great discussion. As seen in some of your earlier threads, Paul.

This one’s especially well traveled ground, though ;). You’ll find some good ones.

posts.name: Fred Bower
posts.cooked:
Yes, producer. Label drinking may not have started in Burgundy (cough…Bordeaux…cough) but it works in Burgundy most of the time if you check your wallet. If you still aren’t convinced, go buy a bargain Grand Cru from an out-of-fashion, reasonably-priced name and get same vintage from a top domaine. Open them side by side and see what happens.

William has posted some interesting pictures of vineyard plots showing the origin of the qualitative differences…it all starts in the vineyard but carries through to the finished product.

Cheers,
fred

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Producer. Only. That’s why there’s $120 GCs and nearly 4 figure village wines.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
Might it depend on the vintage? In a “difficult “ year , definitely producer. (Who may make less wine after they are through selecting before they start their magic)
In a great year maybe grapes over producer.

“Any fool can make DRC wine from DRC fruit, it takes real talent to.do it without” (a fan of Rudy K).

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Hmm, a bit of a different take: “so-so fruit” will not make a great wine even with an outstanding producer. If you mean “great fruit from a so-so site” then yes. There are examples where good producers made negoc wines from sources they didn’t fully control, with average results. Conversely, if you let Charles Lachaux or Dme Leroy grow and harvest your grapes, then even a so-so producer is likely to make excellent wine out of them.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Rauno_E_NZ:

Hmm, a bit of a different take: “so-so fruit” will not make a great wine even with an outstanding producer. If you mean “great fruit from a so-so site” then yes. There are examples where good producers made negoc wines from sources they didn’t fully control, with average results. Conversely, if you let Charles Lachaux or Dme Leroy grow and harvest your grapes, then even a so-so producer is likely to make excellent wine out of them.

I think Rauno hits an important point. Many great producers control both the grape growing and the wine making. They may have grapes from mediocre sites or from lesser vintages but do they have lesser grapes. In the modern world of sciences of grape growing, in many cases no.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Richard Flack:

“Any fool can make DRC wine from DRC fruit, it takes real talent to.do it without” (a fan of Rudy K).

Eh not really. We don’t have a full sample, but we have plenty of so so wines from CC, Batard, Corton, Ech, GE, RSV, even Richebourg and Monty.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
PaulN:

an outstanding producer with so so grapes or a so so producer with outstanding grapes?

That’s the thing: an outstanding producer is outstanding because they grow outstanding grapes (or carefully select outstanding grapes if they are a negociant – see Bruno Giacosa as an example).

If a producer is using so-so grapes to make wine, they are by definition not outstanding. You can’t make chicken salad out of chicken shit, much less chicken Cordon Bleu.

As others say upthread, an outstanding producer can grow outstanding grapes on less lauded terroir (and make outstanding wines from those terroirs).

The better the farmer, the more outstanding the producer.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Yule_Kim:
PaulN:

an outstanding producer with so so grapes or a so so producer with outstanding grapes?

That’s the thing: an outstanding producer is outstanding because they grow outstanding grapes (or carefully select outstanding grapes if they are a negociant – see Bruno Giacosa as an example).

If a producer is using so-so grapes to make wine, they are by definition not outstanding. You can’t make chicken salad out of chicken shit, much less chicken Cordon Bleu.

As others say upthread, an outstanding producer can grow outstanding grapes on less lauded terroir (and make outstanding wines from those terroirs).

The better the farmer, the more outstanding the producer.

We’ll see soon enough with the lamarche wines made by liger belair.

If you compare DRC Corton to the prince Florent de Merode from the year before and DRC CC to the BDM from the year before (same grapes) I think you’ll see some pretty significant improvements, fwiw.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
100% agree.

Though Devil’s Advocate to @Andrew_K There are $120 GC wines because producers aren’t always good and anyone can screw up the best fruit.
But there are four figure village wines because of media, promotion, and FOMO.

Tasted the 2020 Laschaux Echezeaux Les Rouges a week and half ago. It’s a nice wine and I enjoyed it (100% whole cluster, so up my alley). Not remotely worth the money the bottle costs, nor even the best wine that evening. 2020 is an open knit vintage and while I am sure it will evolve and improve with time…I’m disinclined to see it as an earth shaking revelation waiting to happen.

I haven’t had Leroy wines since 1999, and those were young too but they did scream of future potential at the time.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Yule_Kim:

The better the farmer, the more outstanding the producer.

Why do we always devolve to giving people the credit? And then trying to point to one of the people involved as the main reason?

Farming isn’t the be all end all, just look at the great champagnes produced while the town dump was being redistributed in the vineyards 50 years ago.

The truth is pretty simple, it requires site, farmer, and cellar all functioning well to make great wines. Maybe only one of them is required to be a genius-and that can definitely be the site.

When you look at great producers in Burgundy, they usually have a range of sites between village and grand cru. And over 25-30 years, the GC sites usually produce their best wines. From Jadot, yo Rousseau, to Roumier, to Barthod and beyond, this is true. If it was just who farms best, then all their wines would be equally amazing.

And flat out, I have worked with great farmers and less than great farmers, and the site corrects their errors as much as they correct the sites errors.

There’s a reason why people think Koosah vineyard in Oregon is special. 1) because it is. 2) because that makes some of the best producers want to work with it. 3) and the original owners were some of the most knowledgeable farmers in the Valley. But it takes all three. And Jadot buying it hasn’t messed up the farming. Hence the rise of a new vineyard, but the farmers aren’t the ones driving the bus for the vineyard’s reputation.

Edit: I completely whiffed on vine age in my rant here but to not have the age of the vines as part of this is a huge error on my part. Old vines make everyone look good.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

There’s a reason why people think Koosah vineyard in Oregon is special. 1) because it is. 2) because that makes some of the best producers want to work with it. 3) and the original owners were some of the most knowledgeable farmers in the Valley. But it takes all three. And Jadot buying it hasn’t messed up the farming. Hence the rise of a new vineyard, but the farmers aren’t the ones driving the bus for the vineyard’s reputation.

The point I was making is that an outstanding producer is going to use outstanding fruit. They can either farm it (domaine wine) or purchase well-farmed fruit (which is why I cited Giacosa and his negociant wines: his ability to select outstanding fruit made those wines outstanding despite him not farming the grapes).

This was in response to OP saying what is more important “an outstanding producer using so-so grapes” or “a so-so producer using outstanding grapes.” Obviously, the producer needs to know her or his business in the cellar, but if you have substandard fruit, there’s no technical wizardry or manipulation that is going to produce an outstanding wine.

To your point about site, of course site matters, but if someone blasts Romanee Conti with Round Up and takes zero care of the vines, they are not going to get the quality of fruit that will allow a great wine to be made. Fundamentally, you need to farm those sites well to unlock the sites’ potential.

Perhaps I was little strident when I said the best producers are the best farmers because certainly there are good negociants making good wine (though I don’t think it is a coincidence that most of the great wines in Europe are domaine wines), but fundamentally, wine is made in the vineyard first and then in the cellar.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
There’s too big a gap between “blast the vineyard with Round up and doesn’t farm the vines” and you have to farm well.

Too many people don’t realize that an average farmer with a great site is a great farmer. Add in old vines, and an exceptional winemaker and the wines will be great.

Farming with chemicals was popular in the past, and while I hear producers tut-tutting over poor farming techniques then but tye wines seemed to have turned out quite well (in a climate much more rigorous than now, and with many, many,many less options in viticulture and cellar work). Recency bias often proclaims current wines the best ever (along with social media) and some Domaines are better, but there were absolutely amazing Burgundies from the 50s-80s as well.

Regenerative farming is way better, but as I noted, most of the time we try to give credit to the humans regardless of the fact that Burgundy was codified centuries ago and no one really says that codification was incorrect. Though climate change may have the final say.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

There’s too big a gap between “blast the vineyard with Round up and doesn’t farm the vines” and you have to farm well.

Too many people don’t realize that an average farmer with a great site is a great farmer. Add in old vines, and an exceptional winemaker and the wines will be great.

Farming with chemicals was popular in the past, and while I hear producers tut-tutting over poor farming techniques then but tye wines seemed to have turned out quite well (in a climate much more rigorous than now, and with many, many,many less options in viticulture and cellar work). Recency bias often proclaims current wines the best ever (along with social media) and some Domaines are better, but there were absolutely amazing Burgundies from the 50s-80s as well.

Regenerative farming is way better, but as I noted, most of the time we try to give credit to the humans regardless of the fact that Burgundy was codified centuries ago and no one really says that codification was incorrect. Though climate change may have the final say.

I have a great respect for your knowledge and experience and your contributions to this board. But, I do suspect a lot of those great Burgundies of the 50s-80s came from fruit that came from farmers who tended their vines with care and did the best they could to make those grapes as healthy as possible (even if they used some less than ideal chemcials).

There is certainly evidence of great wines being made in vineyards that look like the surface of the Moon (the photo of the Raveneau vineyard in Adventures on on the Wine Route was eye-opening for me), but I also suspect that despite his use of herbicides, Francois Raveneau did do a lot of good things in the vineyard and took conscientious care of his vines to produce those great wines.

Anyway, I do hope we don’t create a takeaway that farming doesn’t matter and all the real work to make wine is done in the cellar. I think we saw what that mindset did during the high modernist/Parker era where people thought they could cut corners by using reverse osmosis and Mega Purple to fix substandard wines rather than focusing on making sure they had good fruit to begin with.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

Why do we always devolve to giving people the credit? And then trying to point to one of the people involved as the main reason?

Farming isn’t the be all end all, just look at the great champagnes produced while the town dump was being redistributed in the vineyards 50 years ago.

The truth is pretty simple, it requires site, farmer, and cellar all functioning well to make great wines. Maybe only one of them is required to be a genius-and that can definitely be the site.

Not sure about that. I go back a fairly long way and can remember times when wines from “great sites” could be pretty mediocre. Things like 1967 Mouton, 1970 Ausone, several Chateau Margauxs from the 1960s and 1970s including the 1971, 1970 Lafite, etc. And, these were not horrible vintages - I have had some wonderful 1970s for example and think of it as a pretty awesome vintage - in the right hands meaning farmer and winemaker. And, then, there were truly horrible vintages in the past like 1972 in Bordeaux.

The same was true in Burgundy. To me, the biggest mistake people make in buying Burgundy is paying a lot of money for grand crus from mediocre producers. Today, there are a lot more excellent winemakers in Burgundy and not just the handful that some here would have you believe. But, there are still pretty mediocre ones.

Yes, today you do not see too many disasters from great sites, but isn’t a lot of that improved technology, which is essence means improved farming.

To me, history has shown that great site is not enough.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

There’s too big a gap between “blast the vineyard with Round up and doesn’t farm the vines” and you have to farm well.

Too many people don’t realize that an average farmer with a great site is a great farmer. Add in old vines, and an exceptional winemaker and the wines will be great.

Farming with chemicals was popular in the past, and while I hear producers tut-tutting over poor farming techniques then but tye wines seemed to have turned out quite well (in a climate much more rigorous than now, and with many, many,many less options in viticulture and cellar work). Recency bias often proclaims current wines the best ever (along with social media) and some Domaines are better, but there were absolutely amazing Burgundies from the 50s-80s as well.

Regenerative farming is way better, but as I noted, most of the time we try to give credit to the humans regardless of the fact that Burgundy was codified centuries ago and no one really says that codification was incorrect. Though climate change may have the final say.

I agree that current farming practices of using minimal chemicals is better than what was done a generation ago, earlier eras (before any chemicals) provided a lot of rot in poorer vintages.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Of course it’s a combination, and I think even the most vocal here would demonstrate support for that position in a different discussion, even if they don’t in this one, because the answer often depends on exactly what question is being asked. How often do we point to maps of Burgundy and say “look where the vineyard is located!” when the question is about why a particular wine over (or sometimes under) performs? Or offer as an explanation that a particular producers vines are located higher up the slope, or with different exposure, than anothers? Those answers suit our questions, they quite obviously seem to explain the difference. Flip it around and you can answer other questions with responses about wine making or farming.

So I think my response to which is more important is “in what situation?” Ideally, you have great materials in the hands of someone who knows what he or she is doing.

The quality of the grapes is the most important factor … and where they were grown (soil/terroir).

Great winemakers can transform these grapes into great wine, less good winemakers waste the potential quality.

It’s a fact that great winemakers make their greatest wines (usually, if the other factors are equal) from their best vineyards with the qualitatively best grapes.

Vintage factors can compromise the quality of the grapes in certain vineyards.

Mean winemakers rarely make great wines, no matter where the grapes are from.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Marcus_Goodfellow:

There are $120 GC wines because producers aren’t always good and anyone can screw up the best fruit.
But there are four figure village wines because of media, promotion, and FOMO.

True. I steer clear of both TBH.

Maybe a better example would have been $120 village wines that are better than the GCs at the same price.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
I haven’t met anyone with a vineyard that doesn’t look to farm their vines with care. My view is biased towards Oregon, where most growers are first generation vineyard owners and many are newer to growing grapes (and often new to growing anything at all). But in the end, there is a huge investment in growing grapes and while I’ve seen some terrible decision making in vineyards, the large majority of growers that I am exposed to are conscientious in the vineyard or savvy enough to get a vineyard management company to do the work for them. I generally work with owner/farmers with more experience if possible.

But vintages vary, and what works well in one vintage can be less effective in the next. As much as it might sound like there is some perfect recipe of farming that creates “the best grapes” every single year, that’s mostly marketing and in general attempts to achieve this in the real world turn out riper wines more often than better wines.

That’s where I think the great farming=great wines runs aground. As much as the Leroy 1999s were densely concentrated balls of potential greatness, and the farming there is a remarkable commitment to pushing for better wines, they started by pushing for great sites.

Great farming is about a farmer beginning with intelligence and talent, then adding in repition and experience with the same site learning it’s quirks and needs within each vintage. Using Clos Rougeard as an example you can see that unknown sites can rise to greatness with the right farmer and winemaker.

But that farmer is as rare as one would expect (and as rare as probability theory would suggest). The large majority of great wines are made with farming that is highly attentive and meticulously follows good protocols for disease prevention. This farmer tries to keep the vines as healthy as possible by following best practices and reacting to unforseen circumstances as quickly as possible.

But from experience, a great site will correct a large number of human errors. And in general is never given credit by anyone involved.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Tasting VCC with Mark Golodetz, I found the most recent iterations to be a disaster. John Gilman, in my opinion, felt the same. Modern high end wines are rarely flawed, but that does not make them good in the least. The 2018-2019 VCC were completely undrinkable to me. That’s true of a lot of top sites for Bordeaux, Napa, and more frequently these days Burgundy as well. So, I 100% do not subscribe to the trope that all modern wines are consistently good. And I would absolutely take the “minefield” of the 80s and 90s over the uniformly safe and over-ripe wines of today.

While there have, and always will be, underachieving wines from any age. Mouton or Lafite floundering in the 70s doesn’t really make my argument incorrect. There were still many excellent wines produced then, you reference the 70 vintage as a whole being solid. But I don’t see site mattering as much in Bordeaux because I don’t know what sites are going into what blend, or whether there was a replant leaving Lafite to deal with younger vines, a failure in the winery as the cellarmaster tried some new technique, or what could have led to Lafite’s struggles with the 70 vintage were (while not drinking that much Bordeaux, I have had the 70 Lafite and it was absolutely as you describe). But on the whole there were still many delicious wines made in Bordeaux in the 70s-most of which I would drink over what is made there today.

And to @Yule_Kim ’s point. Regenerative farming is far and away a better choice than managing a vineyard with a chemical blanket. Organic farming isn’t the fault behind the skyrocketing sugars in modern wines. That’s far more on canopy management, yield management, improved weather forecasting, and oversorting.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Don’t want to get too deep, but I would say this:

  • in the vineyard, you can make the wine better
  • in the winery, beyond giving it a stylistic signature, you can only make it less bad

That’s to say, if you want to increase your inherent potential, you should focus on the vineyard. If you want to realize more of it, you should focus on the winery. Of course, it is advisable from a quality perspective to focus on both.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: That is a singularly unnuanced and ahistorical vision of contemporary Bordeaux. I can understand the impulse to look back with longing to a “golden age”, and most generations of Bordeaux drinkers have done this: Saintsbury was nostalgic for prephyloxera wines, just as Clive Coates was nostalgic for pre-1945 Claret, while some on this forum pine for the wines of the 1980s. But the idea that the 1970s was a golden age really underlines the fact that tastes are ultimately, above all, acquired.

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
I honestly don’t think many of the comments really answered the OP’s question. I used to have this same question and Burg drinkers always say producers, like mantra. But I think the answer should be more nuanced.

Of course, a shitty producer can make a shitty wine out of great grapes, but that’s not really what’s asked here imo.

The OP’s question is comparison between mid producers’ wine out of top grapes vs top producers’ wine out of mid grapes. More like village level Rousseau/Leflaive vs Beze/Chevalier from Faiveley/Olivier Leflaive. Now I would guess the answer would be mid level producer’ top grapes in this example, but I’m probably not experienced enough with Burg to answer confidently, so folks might have differing opinions. And some might think this is unfair example (e.g. if Faiveley is really mid producer or if Rousseau Gevrey is really made of mid fruits). To be clear, this is not to say I think Faiveley and Olivier Leflaive are mid tier producers personally. I think they make great wine but I believe they’re seen as “mediocre” among many Burg drinkers.

Now this is even before we discuss distinction between appellation/fruits. Young and trending winemakers are making great wines from village or regional vineyards, which are “not great sites” in traditional sense, but there’s no guarantee that the traditional appellation is not fallible. It’s more reasonable to think those young producers are making great grapes out of “traditionally neglected vineyards” and thus making great wines. The appellations are becoming more and more irrelevant as the land price is going up and some producers are finding good sites in “lesser” appellations and putting efforts to them as if they were grand cru vineyards. Since many winemakers in Burgundy are growers too, it is quite hard to assume fruit quality based on the appellation nowadays which makes this hypothetical question even harder to answer.

Then there’s a question of price because I assume OP was thinking if he should use his money on the top producer or the top site if he can’t choose both. But I’d stop here as my post is too long already.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
100%.

I wouldn’t say that 70s Bordeaux are a golden age, nor did I.

But there were plenty of great wines made in the 70s.

The ideal that today is some sort of golden age is also mistolaced optimism or simply lacking nuance. There’s plenty of promotional energy begind the wines being currently made, most of which are sognificantly more alcoholic than anything made with regularity in the past. They are not for me, and a taste that I have not acquired.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
TakaoS:

More like village level Rousseau/Leflaive vs Beze/Chevalier from Faiveley/Olivier Leflaive.

Village vs GC is a tougher comparison, but I think 1er from top producers will beat a low level GC every time.

I don’t really chase a lot of village level allocated stuff - if I’m buying a cherry at market price I generally focus on the top wines. I’ve never found a lot of value in stuff like Roumier CM or Rousseau Gevrey, etc at the market price. You pay a lot extra for the name that doesn’t translate and I can find more mid-level producers who scratch the village level itch at a much lower price point. That said, I’m happy to buy and drink the few bottles a year I get at an allocation price as they are well worth those prices.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

Producer. Only. That’s why there’s $120 GCs and nearly 4 figure village wines.

Or you could pour that swill down the drain and drink something good, like Bordeaux.

Sounds like terroir doesn’t matter much in Burgundy?

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
I must say, I did not see that as the OP’s question. If it were, I would expect them to have asked it more in this way. The question of ‘top wine of average producer vs village / bourgogne of a great producer’ has been addressed many times in many threads.

TLDR - producer matters more than vineyard. Of course, this is not universal, e.g. a bretty bottle of 2015 Bourgogne Rouge from Leroy is not better than a 2013 Pruliers from Gavignet.

Instead, the question (as I saw it) was about the quality of the grapes - i.e. combination of site, vine age and health, vintage condition, farming approaches, pruning decisions etc etc, and then harvesting decisions and the immediate aftermath. Quite simply, average producers will virtually never have the highest quality fruit because they are not the best in the vineyard. Even if it’s a “decent” grand cru site.

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
Yeah I don’t know what would be fair/appropriate examples of “so-so” fruits. But to be honest, one could argue low GC and 1er isn’t much different depending on the site. I’ve never had one but maybe something like Mugnier Marechale is “so-so fruits”?

That said, I agree the entry cuvées from the top producers are almost never worth the market price. If the OP is wondering if they should spend $300 on that or a GC from “mid producer”, then the answer is almost always the latter. Or 1er cru from a producer that’s more than “mid tier”. I think this is a common confusion that Burg drinkers cause to novices when they say “producer is the most important”. Many Burg drinkers are rich enough not to put price into equation or they’ve drunk burg long enough to have allocation so that they don’t pay the market price.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
PaulN:

All things being equal who makes a better wine — an outstanding producer with so so grapes or a so so producer with outstanding grapes?

Lots of thoughtful and insightful replies. My reply is skewed towards burgundy.

The 3 big categories of variables that affect wine quality in Burgundy are in my opinion:

  1. Vineyard, this includes the plot of land and the clonal material that is planted on the site.
  2. Farming/grape growing/tending to the vines/attention to detail during the growing and harvesting process
  3. Elevage, this includes when to harvest, how to ferment, pressing, barrel selection, racking etc.

To make an outstanding wine you need all 3 categories to be top notch. It’s debatable whether an outstanding wine can be made if only one or two are top notch.

For example You can have a vineyard like Vosne Malconsorts planted with shitty high yielding clones that’s going to make just ok wine even in the hands of a talented farmer and a top notch producer.

That same vineyard planted to the proper clones still may not result in a great wine if the farming is lazy and indifferent or the elevage is clumsy.

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked: Not quite sure how you interpreted the question. You read it as “which affects the quality of fruits more, producer or appellation”? Then I guess we’re in agreement.

posts.name: Asger L a r s e n
posts.cooked:
Another reflection on the buying strategy for Burgundy - top producer vs. vineyard

Aging of the wine. More modern producers makes wines you can enjoy earlier (e.g. Koji and Charles Lachaux from recent exp). But in general you will not get the full experience if you drink too early. For my palate

  • Village +10 years
  • 1er +15 years
  • GC +20 years

This mean that my buying strategy also takes age into account e.g. I recently purchased and enjoyed Arlaud Clos de la Roche 2002. Arlaud being mid tier. Purchase price in auction was around 250 euro all incl… It was absolutely smoking for my palate - way above recent tastes of village Roumier, Mugnier etc.
So I would rather drink a well selected mid tier aged GC than a top tier village.
posts.display_username: Asger L a r s e n

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Not in Burgundy, as I have discovered to my cost. A good producer will always make a better wine in a ‘bad’ vintage than will a poor producer in a good vintage.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
As people have tossed around the $120 Grand cru as an example of likely mediocrity, I posit the resounding rebuttal: Michel Mallard’s 3 Corton bottlings. Smokin’ good quality at that price. This is rare, but an example of overdelivering Grand crus at very very very fair prices.
Yes, I import them, disclaimer police.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Robert_Panzer:

As people have tossed around the $120 Grand cru as an example of likely mediocrity, I posit the resounding rebuttal: Michel Mallard’s 3 Corton bottlings. Smokin’ good quality at that price. This is rare, but an example of overdelivering Grand crus at very very very fair prices.
Yes, I import them, disclaimer police.

Thank you. I for one appreciate the input of importers like you - it’s one of the only ways most of us will ever hear of these smaller, lesser known producers. We’re capable of taking any potential bias into consideration.
